query: Japan defense strategy revision 2026 Takaichi lethal arms export ban lift

mako.raw:
{"query":"Japan defense strategy revision 2026 Takaichi lethal arms export ban lift","model":"mako","response_time_ms":1685,"verdict":null,"extra_info":null,"results":[{"url":"https://www.iiss.org/online-analysis/online-analysis/2026/04/japan-loosens-the-reins-on-defence-exports/","title":"Japan loosens the reins on defence exports","content":"The Kishida Fumio administration further eased the restrictions in December 2023 through a partial revision of the Three Principles’ Implementation Guidelines, which included making it possible for finished defence products manufactured under licence in Japan to be exported to the licensor country.
For non-lethal equipment there are no export restrictions, while exports of lethal equipment will be permitted to the 17 countries with which Japan has Equipment and Technology Transfer Agreements (ETTAs): Australia, Bangladesh, France, Germany, India, Indonesia, Italy, Malaysia, Mongolia, the Philippines, Singapore, Sweden, Thailand, the United Arab Emirates, the UK, the United States and Vietnam.
Under the new guidelines, the government only needs to notify the Diet regarding a weapons transfer after the National Security Council has judged that it can be approved.
Defence transfers through joint development and procurement, and through licensed production abroad, can enhance the resilience of Japan’s defence supply chains, and increase redundancy, while decentralising production capacity internationally."},{"url":"https://www.csis.org/analysis/japans-new-defense-export-policy-will-industry-seize-day","title":"Japan’s New Defense Export Policy: Will Industry Seize the Day?","content":"On April 21, Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi announced long-anticipated changes to policy governing defense exports, effectively eliminating restrictions on sales—including lethal weapons—to Japan’s closest security partners.
That list currently includes Australia and several European partners; much of Southeast Asia, as well as Bangladesh and India; and the United Arab Emirates.
The Japanese government will release a new defense industrial strategy along with the government’s revised National Security and Defense Strategies to be announced in December 2026."},{"url":"https://www.ecns.cn/m/video/2026-05-12/detail-ihfenirv8000204.shtml","title":"Japan accelerates remilitarization with lift of lethal arms export ban: expert","content":"However, Japan’s April 21 decision to scrap rules that limit Japan's defense equipment exports to five noncombat categories effectively ends the 1976 ban."},{"url":"https://www.pbs.org/newshour/world/japan-lifts-ban-on-lethal-weapons-exports-in-major-change-of-its-postwar-pacifist-policy","title":"Japan lifts ban on lethal weapons exports in major change of its postwar pacifist policy | PBS News","content":"TOKYO (AP) — Japan on Tuesday scrapped a ban on lethal weapons exports, a major change in its postwar pacifist policy as the country seeks to build up its arms industry amid worries over Chinese and North Korean aggression.
Japan began to export some non-lethal military supplies in 2014, and in December 2023 it approved a change that would allow sales of dozens of lethal weapons and components that it manufactures under licenses from other countries back to the licensors, clearing the way for Japan to sell U.S.-designed Patriot missiles to America to make up for munitions that Washington sent to Ukraine.
Dozens of former defense contractors have withdrawn from the market."},{"url":"https://www.cnbc.com/2026/04/21/japan-defense-war-middle-east-ukraine-china.html","title":"Japan scraps ban on lethal weapons exports in pacifist policy shift","content":"Japan’s government has scrapped restrictions on overseas arms sales in a major shift from pacifist policies introduced after World War II.
The Japanese government has announced plans to lift a ban on lethal weapons exports, marking the latest shift away from the country's post-war pacifist policy."}]}

tavily.raw: {"query":"Japan defense strategy revision 2026 Takaichi lethal arms export ban lift","follow_up_questions":null,"answer":null,"images":[],"results":[{"url":"https://www.npr.org/2026/04/21/g-s1-118178/japan-ban-lethal-weapons-exports","title":"Japan approves scrapping a ban on lethal weapons exports : NPR","content":"**Japan approves scrapping a ban on lethal weapons exports** **The approval clears a final set of hurdles for Japan's postwar arms sales and facilitate its future sale of weapons such as a next-generation fighter jet and combat drones.**. # Japan approves scrapping a ban on lethal weapons exports. TOKYO — Japan on Tuesday endorsed scrapping a ban on lethal weapons exports, a major change of its postwar pacifist policy as the country seeks to build up its arms industry and deepen cooperation with defense partners. The approval by Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi's Cabinet of the new guideline clears a final set of hurdles for Japan's postwar arms sales and facilitate its future sale of weapons such as a next-generation fighter jet and combat drones. In 2014, Japan began to export some non-lethal military supplies, and in December 2023 it approved a change that would allow sales of dozens of lethal weapons and components that it manufactures under licenses from other countries back to the licensors such as the United States.","score":0.8668671,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.iiss.org/online-analysis/online-analysis/2026/04/japan-loosens-the-reins-on-defence-exports","title":"Japan loosens the reins on defence exports","content":"On 21 April 2026, the cabinet of Japanese Prime Minister Takaichi Sanae revised the rules restricting Japan's exports of defence equipment","score":0.8390101,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.asahi.com/ajw/articles/16513588","title":"Japan reverses landmark policy to allow lethal arms exports | The Asahi Shimbun: Breaking News, Japan News and Analysis","content":"Photo/Illutration *Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi attends a Cabinet meeting on April 21. Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi's Cabinet on April 21 lifted Japan's decades-old ban on exporting lethal weapons, reversing a cornerstone of the nation's post-war policy. The government approved the change by revising its arms export principles at a National Security Council meeting, abolishing previous rules that limited transfers to non-lethal purposes such as rescue and surveillance. On the same day, Takaichi stated in a post on X, formerly Twitter, “Meeting needs and carrying out defense equipment transfers will contribute to improving the defense capabilities of various countries ... leading to the securing of Japan’s own security.”. Japan first adopted a ban on arms exports to the communist bloc and other destinations in 1967. This was partially eased in 2014 under the Cabinet of Prime Minister Shinzo Abe that created the Three Principles on Transfer of Defense Equipment and Technology allowing exports under certain conditions.","score":0.82728493,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.japantimes.co.jp/news/2026/02/25/japan/defense-exports-rules-revision","title":"Japan takes step closer to easing rules on export of lethal weapons","content":"2026 Japan on Wednesday moved one step closer to allowing the export of lethal arms, approved a proposal rules limiting defense equipment","score":0.81489426,"raw_content":null},{"url":"https://www.lemonde.fr/en/international/article/2026/04/22/japan-lifts-ban-on-lethal-arms-exports_6752730_4.html","title":"Japan lifts ban on lethal arms exports","content":"Japan, moving further away from its strict commitment to pacifism, has ended its ban on exporting lethal weapons abroad.","score":0.8080827,"raw_content":null}],"response_time":0.93,"request_id":"a3b91a7b-ec71-49ae-92d9-b056e7c2f128"}
